description: Iterative interrogative technique that asks 'why' repeatedly (typically five times) to peel away layers of symptoms and reveal the root cause of a problem
when_to_use: When a problem has an unknown or hidden root cause; when you need a simple low-cost RCA tool; as part of Kaizen or lean problem-solving
source_name: Toyota Production System / Taiichi Ohno (1988) Toyota Production System: Beyond Large-Scale Production

description: Visual cause-and-effect diagram that organizes potential causes of a problem into categories (e.g. 6Ms: Man Machine Method Material Measurement Mother Nature) branching off a central spine resembling a fishbone
when_to_use: When a problem has many interacting causes; when a team needs to visually map all contributing factors; one of the Seven Basic Tools of Quality
source_name: Kaoru Ishikawa - University of Tokyo (1960s); EBSCO Research Starter on Ishikawa Diagram

description: Framework for organizing information so that categories do not overlap (mutually exclusive) and together they cover the entire problem space (collectively exhaustive); central to the Minto Pyramid Principle
when_to_use: When structuring analysis presentations or data classification; when breaking down a problem into sub-components; foundational to McKinsey-style consulting communication
source_name: Barbara Minto - The Pyramid Principle (McKinsey & Company 1970s)

description: Reasoning method that breaks a complex problem down to its most fundamental axiomatic truths that cannot be deduced further then rebuilds understanding from the ground up rather than by analogy
when_to_use: When challenging entrenched assumptions; when innovating in a domain dominated by conventional wisdom; when designing from physics fundamentals rather than existing solutions
source_name: Aristotle (Physics Metaphysics); Elon Musk popularized in modern engineering (SpaceX Tesla interviews)

description: Disciplined systematic questioning method that probes assumptions evidence implications and counterarguments through six categories: clarification assumptions reasoning perspectives implications and the question itself
when_to_use: When examining the validity of a claim or belief; when teaching critical thinking; when uncovering hidden assumptions in an argument
source_name: Socrates (as recorded by Plato); Linda Elder & Richard Paul - The Thinker's Guide to the Art of Socratic Questioning (Foundation for Critical Thinking 2016)

description: Hierarchical taxonomy of cognitive learning objectives (Remember Understand Apply Analyze Evaluate Create) used to structure questions at increasing levels of complexity and critical thinking depth
when_to_use: When designing questions that range from recall to evaluation; when assessing depth of understanding; when scaffolding inquiry from basic to higher-order thinking
source_name: Benjamin Bloom et al. (1956); revised by Anderson & Krathwohl (2001) - A Taxonomy for Learning Teaching and Assessing

description: Framework for formulating answerable clinical questions: Patient/Population Intervention Comparison and Outcome; standard structure for evidence-based medicine literature searches
when_to_use: When formulating clinical or intervention research questions; when searching medical databases (PubMed Cochrane); when designing RCTs or systematic reviews in healthcare
source_name: Richardson WS et al. (1995) ACP Journal Club; Cochrane Handbook Chapter 2

description: Mnemonic for evaluating research questions: Feasible Interesting Novel Ethical and Relevant; used to assess whether a research question is worth pursuing before investing resources
when_to_use: When evaluating candidate research questions before committing to a study; when reviewing grant proposals or thesis topics; at the inception of a research design
source_name: Hulley SB Cummings SR Browner WS Grady DG Newman TB - Designing Clinical Research (Lippincott Williams & Wilkins)

description: Framework for qualitative/mixed-methods questions: Sample Phenomenon of Interest Design Evaluation and Research type; alternative to PICO designed specifically for non-quantitative evidence synthesis
when_to_use: When formulating qualitative or mixed-methods research questions; when conducting qualitative systematic reviews; when PICO is too intervention-focused for the research design
source_name: Cooke A Smith D Booth A (2012) Beyond PICO: The SPIDER tool for qualitative evidence synthesis. Qualitative Health Research 22(10) 1435-1443

description: Consolidated Standards of Reporting Trials: checklist-based guideline for reporting parallel-group randomized controlled trials ensuring transparency of design methods results and analysis
when_to_use: When reporting results of a randomized controlled trial; when submitting RCT manuscripts to medical journals; when appraising RCT reporting quality
source_name: Schulz KF Altman DG Moher D - CONSORT Group (2010) BMJ 340:c332

description: Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses: evidence-based minimum item set for reporting systematic reviews including flow diagram for study screening and selection
when_to_use: When reporting a systematic review or meta-analysis; when submitting review manuscripts; updated from PRISMA 2009 to PRISMA 2020 with expanded checklist
source_name: Page MJ McKenzie JE Bossuyt PM et al. (2021) The PRISMA 2020 statement. BMJ 372:n71
